chapters.summary: Menin previews resolutions commemorating NYC's role in the birth of the US (Gennaro), historical documents at NYPL (Marte), African descent instruction in schools (Joseph), Freedom Day on July 2 (Ariola), and the 250th anniversary of the United States (Menin and Carr).
chapters.title: Speaker Menin agenda overview - 250th anniversary and Freedom Day resolutions

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thank you to Christopher Sartori and Patrick Mulvihill.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: In honor of the United States of America's 250th anniversary, we will also pass a slate of resolutions.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Pre-considered resolutions sponsored by Councilmember Jim Gennaro would commemorate the pivotal political and military role

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: of New York City in the birth of the United States 250 years ago.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thank you to Regina Paul.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Pre-considered resolutions sponsored by Councilmember Christopher-Marte would celebrate the historical documents that contributed to the birth of the United States of America and are on display at the New York Public Library as part of the nation's

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: 250th anniversary events.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thank you to Regina Paul.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Pre-considered resolutions sponsored by Councilmember Rita Joseph would call on the New York State Legislature to pass and the Governor to sign legislation to amend the education law to require instruction on the history,

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: contributions, and experiences of people of African descent in public schools.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thank you to Chloe Rivera.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Resolution 15, sponsored by Councilmember Joann-Ariola, would declare July 2nd annually in the City of New York as Freedom Day

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: to commemorate the anniversary of the vote of the Second Continental Congress to declare independence from Great Britain in 1776.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thank you to Regina Paul.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And Resolution 5, sponsored by myself and Minority Leader David Carr, would commemorate the 250th anniversary of the United States of America.

chapters.summary: Menin previews Intro 956 renaming 103 thoroughfares (Abreu), Intro 859-A on street name biographical information (Brewer), and Intro 92-A permitting DSNY to place stickers on vehicles for alternate side parking enforcement (Brewer).
chapters.title: Speaker Menin agenda overview - Street renaming omnibus and sanitation bills

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thank you to Regina Paul.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Pre-considered introductions sponsored by Majority Leader Shaun Abreu would rename 103 thoroughfares and public places in our city.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thank you to Chris Sartori, Patrick Mulvihill, Michael Sherman,

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: and Owen Katowski.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Introduction 859-A, sponsored by Councilmember Gail Brewer,

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: would specify the process to be used by the Department of Records and Information Services to comply with existing law,

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: requiring the posting of biographical information for the subjects of name changes of streets and parks when no biographical information can be found in the legislative record.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thank you to Chris Sartori, Patrick Mulvihill, Michael Sherman, and Owen Katowski.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Introduction 92A, also sponsored by Councilmember Brewer, would permit DOT and the Department of Sanitation and any department designated by DSNY to place a sticker, no larger,

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: an 8.5 by 11 inches to be specific, on the window of any motor vehicle as a method of enforcing alternate side parking rules.

chapters.summary: Menin previews Intro 580-A creating an office of child care (Gutierrez), Intro 31-A expanding commercial organic waste requirements (Abreu), and Intro 15-A limiting redundant child care background checks (Abreu).
chapters.title: Speaker Menin agenda overview - Child care, organic waste, and background check bills

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thank you to Morgan Barrett.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Introduction 580A, sponsored by Councilmember Jennifer Gutierrez, would create an office of childcare and early childhood education.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: To work with the mayor in planning and implementing child care for any child who resides in the city at no cost to a parent, step-parent, guardian, or relative.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thank you to Julia Goldsmith Pinkham.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Introduction 31A, sponsored by Majority Leader Abreu, would expand the categories of establishments that can be designated by DSNY to be subject to requirements regarding separation and disposal

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: of organic waste.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: It also required DSNY to designate additional covered establishments by December 1st, 2026.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thank you to Morgan Barrett and Audrey San.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Introduction 15-A, also sponsored by Majority Leader Abrea, would prohibit DOHMH from acquiring a subsequent background check of an individual for the purposes of working in a center-based child care program if

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: DOHMH has already completed a background check unless the individual has not been employed by a child care provider in the city for more than 180 consecutive days in the past five years,

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: a child care program requests a background check, or a background check is otherwise required by law or necessary for purposes of safety.

chapters.summary: Menin previews Intro 929-A requiring NYPD transparency reports on security perimeters at educational facilities (Encarnación), Intro 553-A creating a small retail business security system program (Feliz), and Intro 910-A repealing security grill visibility requirements (Stevens).
chapters.title: Speaker Menin agenda overview - Public safety and small business bills

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thank you to Julia Goldsmith Pinkham.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Introduction 929-A, sponsored by Deputy Whip Elsie-Encarnación, would require the police commissioner to publish a transparency report

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: on the New York Police Department's plan to address and contain the risk of physical obstruction, physical injury, intimidation, and interference at entrances to and exits from certain educational facilities in the city, including through the use of security perimeters.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thank you to Rachel Codrero, Jessica Boulay, and Josh Kingsley.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Introduction 553-A, sponsored by Councilmember Oswald Feliz, would require that the Department of Small Business Services or another agency as designated by the mayor establish a small retail business security.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: system program to provide financial assistance to owners of small retail businesses that would reduce the cost of purchasing and installing security system technology.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thank you to Tyler Walls and Rebecca Barilla.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Introduction 910-8 sponsored by Councilmember Althea Stevens would repeal the requirement originally set

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: in Local Law 75 of 2009, that as of July 2026, all security grills installed in Group B or M buildings permit visibility of at least 70% of the area covered by the grill.

chapters.summary: Menin previews the pre-considered introduction by Council Member Pierina Sanchez establishing a new rental assistance voucher program for families and individuals, operated by HPD, with eligibility for households at or below 50% AMI.
chapters.title: Speaker Menin agenda overview - Rental assistance voucher program (Intro 966)

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thank you to Tyler Walls, Rebecca Barilla, Austin Malone, Claudie Bennett, William Eck, Jerk Spencer, and Jose Conde.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And lastly, and certainly last but not least, pre-considered introduction sponsored by Councilmember Pierina Sanchez would establish a new

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: rental assistance voucher program for families and individuals which would be subject to appropriation and operated by HPD.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: To be eligible for the program, households would have a total income that does not exceed 50% of the AMI, as well as meet additional eligibility requirements based on the type of shelter or housing in which they reside.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thank you to Andrea Vasquez, Samita Deshmukh, Nicholas Connell, Jonathan Rosenberg, and Julia Haramis.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Okay, that was it.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thank you for your attention.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I'm going to turn it back over to our majority leader.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Thank you, Speaker Menon.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: I'll move into discussion of general orders, beginning with Councilor Pierina Sanchez, followed by Narcisse.

chapters.summary: Council Member Sanchez thanks Speaker Menin, the Progressive Caucus, and advocates for the CityFEPs settlement. She details the $300 million commitment across FY27-28, expansion to non-DHS shelters including runaway youth and fire-displaced families, eviction prevention for rent-stabilized tenants, and no work requirements for newly eligible New Yorkers.
chapters.title: Pierina Sanchez on historic CityFEPs settlement and voucher expansion

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you, Majority Leader.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: My apologies.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you, Majority Leader.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Before I begin, I want to thank my colleagues, especially Speaker Menon, for your leadership holding the line on this historic win for low-income New Yorkers in our city.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: I want to thank the Progressive Caucus for uplifting this priority, to the Council's leadership for holding the line, and all the Council members, to advocates for never giving up on New Yorkers that they represent, and to the Mayor and his team for staying at the table and getting this to the finish line.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: I'm going to pull a Tiffany Cabon and stand up.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Today's settlement, see she even shows me how, today's settlement, the fiscal year 27 budget and this legislation represent a historic agreement that will finally expand housing vouchers for New Yorkers facing homelessness.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: This is a historic win for vulnerable New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: We're expanding access responsibly, controlling costs, and beginning to shift the Titanic, moving the city away from costly shelter reliance and toward permanent homes.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Because the status quo does not work, our city must stop paying more for worse outcomes.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: It costs $54 a day to keep a family housed with a city FEPS voucher compared to $270 a day.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: For a family shelter bed.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: And behind those numbers are children and families.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: 70% of shelter residents are families with children.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Thousands of babies, 20,000 babies born in shelter just last year.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: With $300 million committed across fiscal year 27 and 28, the new program established by Intro 966 will reach New Yorkers excluded from the current CityFEP program,

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: including households in non-DOH shelters, meaning runaway and homeless youth, families displaced by fires or vacate orders, and justice-involved New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: And will support eviction prevention for income-eligible tenants in rent-stabilized homes.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: There will be no work requirement for newly eligible New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Homelessness itself is enough of a barrier.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: And this is responsible governance.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Funding will be distributed between shelter exits and eviction prevention administered.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Please wrap up.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: We're creating this program because the choice is not between compassion and fiscal discipline.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: The council can and must do both.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: That's what we're doing.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Councilmember Narcisse, followed by Hudson.

chapters.summary: Council Member Narcisse thanks Speaker Menin for holding the line on CityFEPs and addresses immigration inequities. She honors street co-naming recipients including John Linder of Linder's Pork Store, volunteer EMT Fred Wilkins, teacher Audrey Duss, and cancer advocate Gianna Gambino, whose family is present.
chapters.title: Narcisse on CityFEPs and street co-namings in District 28

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Thank you, Speaker Menon, for listening when it comes to CityFed and holding the line for real.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: I truly believe that folks belong in an apartment, not in shelter.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Shelter should be a transitional process, so thank you for that.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And thank you for all your leadership, your team that responded.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And when I call, because I go crazy when people are calling me.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And answering the inequities is very important at a time, is a challenging time in immigration.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: I'm happy for that, too.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And, of course, the street co-naming in my district is so important.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Those are folks that work all their lives.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: They need to be recognized.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: John Linder ran the legendary Linder's Pork Store on Avenue N for over 60 years.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: The same store the father opened back in 1958 and never let the neighbor go without, you know, supporting them.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Never let anyone go hungry.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: That's very important.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Fred Wilkins spent almost 40 years answering the call as a volunteer in a Flatland Volunteer Ambulance Corps, training EMTs and saving lives right here in our community.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: So thank you.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Aunt Audrey Duss taught at PS91 for 32 years and never stopped giving after her retirement, 50 years volunteering at Downstate Medical Center.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Thousands of hats knit by hands for the kids in the community.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And I want to take a moment with the next one because Gianna Gambino, mom is here.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Her sisters and her friends are here to support.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And I want to say thank you because they took the pain and they transformed it into helping the community with folks that are going through a cancer process.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: She was very young, but she never stopped fighting for others.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: She understood the mission.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: and the vision to help one another.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: As a registrar, seeing people suffering with cancer, seeing that young woman doing that, it is amazing.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Never too early to be a leader and a hero.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And it's you.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: So well deserved.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And I want to stop right here to say thank you to all my colleagues.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: We fought hard for this budget with the speaker and of course, Chair Lee.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Councilmember Hudson, followed by Banks.

chapters.summary: Council Member Hudson honors Edith Mott Young, Bishop Carl E. Williams, Reverend Clinton M. Miller, and the Panamanian community of Brooklyn with street co-namings. Delta Sigma Theta members are in the balcony, and Panamanian Consul General Roosevelt Thayer is recognized.
chapters.title: Hudson on street co-namings in District 35 including Little Panama

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: I would like to take a moment to recognize the four individuals and communities in District 35 that we're honoring in today's co-naming bill.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Edith Mott Young, Bishop Carl E.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Williams, Reverend Clinton M.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Miller, and the Panamanian community of Brooklyn.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: These co-namings are about more than street signs.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: They ensure that the people and communities who help shape our neighborhoods are permanently reflected in the places they call home.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Edith Mott Young devoted her life to education and uplifting young people, leaving a lasting impact on generations of Brooklyn students and black women as one of the 22 founders of Delta Sigma Theta sorority.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Members of Delta Sigma Theta are with us in the balcony today.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Bishop Carl E.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Williams was a beloved faith leader whose ministry and community service touched countless lives in my district and beyond.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Reverend Clinton M.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Miller was a pillar of Clinton Hill whose decades of leadership at Brown Memorial Baptist Church extended far beyond its walls.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: He was a trusted advocate, mentor, and voice.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: For justice whose legacy continues to shape the community today.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: And I also want to recognize the designation of Little Panama along Franklin Avenue.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: For generations, Crown Heights

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Has been home to a thriving Panamanian community whose culture, businesses, and traditions have enriched our borough.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: This co-naming is a celebration of that enduring legacy and an acknowledgement that their contributions are an essential part of Brooklyn's story.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: I'd also like to thank Panamanian Consulate General Roosevelt Thayer, who was here today.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Viva Panama.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: And I'd like to thank the families, advocates, and community members who worked to make these long overdue recognitions possible.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Councilor Banks, followed by Epstein.

chapters.summary: Council Member Banks honors two women whose lives were defined by service: Donna Joaquina Jones, director of the Louis H. Pink Houses Community Center, and Deidre Cheatham, a council staff member and advocate for sexual assault survivors and public housing residents. Their families are in the balcony.
chapters.title: Banks on street co-namings honoring Donna Joaquina Jones and Deidre Cheatham

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Thank you, Majority Leader, and thank you to the Speaker for her leadership as well.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Today we have the opportunity to honor the two incredible women whose lives were defined by service, compassion, and unwavering commitment to others, Donna Joaquina Jones and Deidre Cheatham.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: I believe their families are on the balcony today.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Donna Joaquina Jones devoted her life to the Pink Houses Community Center as a director of the Louis H.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Pink Houses Community Center.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: She created opportunities for young people, supported families, and became someone countless residents could depend on.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: She believed every child deserved a chance to succeed, and she spent her entire life making sure that they had one.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Her legacy lives on through the generations of young people she encouraged and mentored and inspired.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Now, Deidre Cheatham shared the same heart for service, whether she was serving her

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: her New York City, serving in the New York City Council, or working with community organizations, or advocating for survivors of sexual assault, or helping families in need, Deidre always showed up for people.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: She led with kindness, treated everyone with respect, and made a difference in both big and small ways.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: So many people remembered not just what she accomplished, but how she made them feel.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: I had the privilege of working alongside both Donna and Deidre, and I witnessed their commitment firsthand.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Whether they were raising awareness for autism, advocating for public housing residents, or helping advance initiatives that benefited the 42nd Council District,

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: they approached every task with compassion, professionalism, and a genuine desire to help others.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: When you think about Donna and Deidre, one thing becomes clear.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: They made their communities better simply by being a part of them.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: They gave their time, their energy, and their hearts to helping others, and they left behind legacies that will continue to inspire us all.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Please wrap up.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Today, we prepared to permanently honor them through these street co-namings, and we are ensuring that their future generations will know their names and understand the lasting impact they made to our community.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Councilmember Epstein, followed by Brewer.

chapters.summary: Council Member Epstein thanks the Speaker and Finance Chair for the historic budget, highlighting CityFEPs, 1,100 NYCHA units back online, supportive housing, DCWP funding, containerization, Fair Fares, and the workers' dust initiative.
chapters.title: Epstein on budget wins for NYCHA, supportive housing, and workers

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Good evening.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I want to thank the speaker and the finance chair for really

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: This historic moment.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: We think about what we did around FEPS and the thousands of families that we're going to protect.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: For NYCHA to get the 6,000 units that are offline right now, to get another 1,100 back online,

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: The supportive housing units, the increased funding in DCWP, containerization, fair fares, workers'dust initiative, pet food pantries, spay-neuter, time and time again.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: This council stood up for working New Yorkers, stood up for affordability, and this budget reflects those values.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: This is an important day in the city to say that we care deeply about this city.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: We care about people who live here, and this council is fighting for them.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: This is a really important moment, and I want to thank the speaker again for her leadership and to know that a budget like this reflects our values, that we stood up for everyday New Yorkers and will continue to.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: I would like to remind folks that if, for whatever reason, you can't get your marks done through general orders, you can do it during the time that you vote.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: So if I cut you off, I'm sorry about that.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Just make sure you bring it up later on if you need to.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Councilmember Brewer, followed by Councilmember Brooks-Powers.

chapters.summary: Council Member Brewer explains Intro 92A restoring DSNY stickers for alternate side parking violations, noting 500,000 cars violate rules weekly. She also explains Intro 859A requiring DORIS to post biographical information for 9/11 first responder street co-namings, and honors Cecile Richards with a co-naming at 83rd Street and Central Park West.
chapters.title: Brewer on Intro 92A (alternate side stickers) and Intro 859A (street name bios)

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Intro 92A, alternate side of the street stickers.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: In 1987, sanitation started placing day glow green stickers on vehicles with repeated alternate side of the street violations.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: The council banned them in 2012.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I probably voted for that stupid bill.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And people make the following calculation.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: It's cheaper to not move your car for alternate side of the street parking and risk paying the $65 parking ticket cheaper than a garage.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: In April of this year, the Sanitation Department estimated 500,000 cars violated street cleaning regulations.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: In 1,200 routes every single week.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: That meant that 3,000 miles was not cleaned.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: People need to move their damn car.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And so I want to thank Speaker Menon, Sanitation Chair Sanchez for their support, Morgan Barrett, Joanna Castro, Andrew Velasquez, Jeff Baker, and for my office, Cynthia Hornig and Shula Puder.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I do want to thank the Sanitation Department also.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Number two, 859A.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: This has to do with Omnibus Street naming.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: We have a long history.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: We want to make sure people know about it.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And in 2023, the council passed my local law 104, requiring the City Department of Records and Information Services, DARS, to create an interactive digital map of these co-namings.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: But between 2002 and 2003, I think some of you were alive then, more than 400 streets were co-named for first responders killed on 9-11.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: The speaker and I went to an awful lot of funerals then, and the city lacked their biographical information to update the map.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: To the credit of Doris and former Commissioner Pauline Toole, they found all...

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: 400 people wrote up their bios and put them through the council.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: That's what this law does.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: It says this, these are 400 people, they're ready to be recorded.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I want to thank Council Chris Santori and Senior Legislative Patrick Moff for their work.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I just want to also mention Cecile Richards.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Cecile Richards Way is one of the considering for co-signing at 83rd Street in Central Park West, head of Planned Parenthood, daughter of Ann Richards, governor.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Councilor Brooks-Powers, followed by Maloney.

chapters.summary: Council Member Brooks-Powers thanks Council Member Hudson for sponsoring the co-naming for SOAR founder Edith Mott Young, recognizing her impact across Brooklyn and the world.
chapters.title: Brooks-Powers on Edith Mott Young street co-naming

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I just wanted to take a moment to thank my colleague, Councilmember Hudson, for sponsoring the co-name for my SOAR founder, Edith Mott Young, and recognizing

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: her impactful life, not only to the Brooklyn community, but really across the world.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And so I'm really excited that I get to vote on this street co-name for my SOARs in Brooklyn.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: So thank you for that.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember Brooks-Powers.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Councilmember Maloney, followed by Hankerson.

chapters.summary: Council Member Maloney introduces co-namings for Jewish Community Relations Council Way at Dag Hammarskjold Plaza, Patrolman Kenneth Keller at East 67th Street, and philanthropist Joan Davidson at Lexington and 75th Street. She also congratulates her office's first intern on graduating.
chapters.title: Maloney on three East Side street co-namings

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Thank you, Majority Leader.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: I'm proud to introduce three street co-namings honoring people and institutions that have shaped our Eastside community.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: The first, Jewish Community Relations Council Way, will be at 2nd Avenue and East 40th.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: 7Th Street next to Dag Hammarskjold Plaza.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: JCRC New York has built relationships across New York's Jewish community and worked for a more interconnected city for all ethnic and religious groups for 50 years.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Its headquarters once sat just south of the plaza, making it the organization's local park and a frequent site of rallies, protests, and press conferences it organized or supported.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: The second corrects an existing sign at East 67th Street and Lexington Avenue, adding the rank patrolman to honor Kenneth Keller of the 19th Precinct, an officer who fell in the line of duty,

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: ensuring that his service and sacrifice are properly recognized.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And third honors Joan Davidson at Lexington and 75th Street, the corner where she last lived.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: A devoted philanthropist, Davidson chaired the New York State Council on the Arts in the 1970s and served as a state park commissioner in the late 1990s, leaving a lasting mark on our state's culture and parks.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: I'm honored to bring these co-namings forward, and I also want to recognize and congratulate our very first intern, Key, on graduating both from college and from our office.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: We're incredibly proud of these milestones and grateful for her contributions.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Councilmember Hankerson, followed by Stevens.

chapters.summary: Council Member Hankerson celebrates the historic $29.1 million baseline for parks, ending the annual budget dance for GreenThumb, tree stump removal, urban park rangers, recreation staff, and PEP officers. He thanks Speaker Menin and pledges to continue fighting for 1% parks funding.
chapters.title: Hankerson on historic $29.1 million parks funding baseline

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: Thank you, Majority Leader.

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: Today is a proud day for our parks, our communities, and every parkie and park abbot.

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: who keeps the city thriving.

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: In this budget, we restored and secured additional funding for green thumb, tree stump removal, tree planting, urban park rangers, recreation staff, and our beloved PEP officers.

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: These are not just line items.

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: These are the people who open our parks, care for our trees, keep our recreation centers active,

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: support our gardens, protect our public spaces, and make sure New Yorkers can use the parks they love.

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: For the past few years, after every budget dance, the council, parks, and advocates have had to leave saying, we secured the jobs for another year, take a rest, and get ready to do it all over again.

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: But not this year.

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: This year, we are proud to have accomplished a historic budget resulting in a $29.1 million baseline, which is so much greater than breathing a temporary sigh of relief.

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: Together, as a city, we are building a stronger foundation.

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: So to every Parkie who has ever questioned whether your city cares for you,

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: Let this be the marker that demonstrates that we understand you are valuable.

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: Your work is seen, your labor is appreciated, and that your city deems you as essential.

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: I want to thank Speaker Menon, my colleagues in the administration for their outstanding commitment to our parkies and the communities they serve.

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: This is a major step, but we are not done.

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: We will not let up until we get to the 1%.

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Council Member Stevens explains Intro 910A repeals Local Law 75 of 2009's requirement that businesses replace security grills at costs exceeding $10,000. She thanks Shanel Thomas-Henry for chairing the hearing and emphasizes the Council's commitment to reducing unnecessary burdens on small businesses.
chapters.title: Stevens on Intro 910A repealing security grill mandate

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Councilmember Stevens, followed by Gutierrez.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Today I'm proud to

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I'm honored to have the support of the majority of my colleagues in passing of 910A.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: As a member of council, it's our responsibility to protect the small businesses that are the backbone of New York City's economy.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: They create jobs, provide essential services, and help define the characters of our neighborhoods.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: When the government creates policies that are unintentionally placed, unnecessary burdens on those businesses, it is also our responsibility to correct them.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: That is exactly what we are doing today.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: The implementation of Law 75 of 2009 was expected to replace an unnecessary financial burden on business owners across the city by requiring many of them to replace security grills

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: that were already safe, secure, or fully functional.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: For some businesses, those replacements would have cost exceeded $10,000.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: At this time, our small business continue to face rising rent costs, inflation increasing, operating costs, and government should not be adding unnecessary expenses that threaten their ability to succeed.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Intro 910A repels an outdated mandate that while continuing to allow businesses owners to maintain safe, secure storefronts.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: More importantly, it demonstrates that this council is listening to the concerns of our small businesses, communities, and taking meaningful action to support them.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I want to thank my colleague, and special shout out to Shanel-Thomas-Henry, who chaired the hearing, my staff, the bids, all my colleagues who supported this legislation.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Your partnerships made today's vote possible.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: To every small business across the five boroughs, this vote sends a clear message.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: This council is committed to reducing unnecessary burdens, supporting economic opportunities, and ensuring that New York City remains a place where your business can grow and thrive.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Councilmember Gutierrez, followed by Caban.

chapters.summary: Council Member Gutierrez celebrates the passage of Intro 580A creating the Mayor's Office of Child Care, thanking sponsors including Council Member Riley and Speaker Menin. She notes the office will hold the city accountable on child care deserts and provider support regardless of who is mayor.
chapters.title: Gutierrez on Intro 580A codifying Office of Child Care

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Thank you so much, Majority Leader.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I'm really excited to turn the chapter on a very exciting package of bills.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I think this is the last bill part of that original package, codifying the Mayor's Office of Child Care to help us as a city get to a true universal child care program that we can all be proud of.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I want to thank every single person who was a sponsor three times that we reintroduced it, including Councilmember Kevin Riley from the Bronx.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I want to thank, of course, Speaker Julie-Menin, who has been a partner on all things universal child care since before becoming a speaker.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: And this is a really, really exciting opportunity because not only are we going to be able to say, to hold the city accountable when it comes to child care, child care deserts, the way that they are supported, the way that we support providers,

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: but it's going to stand the test of time, right?

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: It doesn't matter who is at City Hall, the Mayor's Office of Child Care will be there to work with every single agency necessary, city and state, to make it happen, to make it accessible for every single New Yorker,

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: regardless of immigration status and regardless of income.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: New York has a real opportunity to lead, and I'm so excited that we get to be a part of that council that is doing that.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Very quickly, I want to thank my whole legislative team, everyone that helped us get here.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I want to thank my former chief of staff, Anya Lair, who I joked with her, said she was not pregnant when we reintroduced it, and now she's had her baby now that universal child care plan has passed.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: So congrats to her, yes.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: And of course, I want to thank all of my colleagues who have joined us at all the rallies, to all the providers who have come to testify, who are very much leaning on us to help them get to dignified wages and dignified benefits.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: We are still in that struggle.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: We are still in that struggle.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: But I want to thank everyone here who showed up for working families.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: And that's it.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Councilmember Cavan, followed by Morano.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Council Member Cabán reserves budget comments for the vote, but states deep reservations about Intro 929 potentially silencing dissent, notes Intro 553 duplicates an underutilized state program, and thanks budget staff.
chapters.title: Cabán on reservations about Intro 929 and Intro 553

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I plan to reserve my comments on the budget for during that vote.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Regarding Intro 929, I continue to hold deep reservations that I had for Intro 175B, that this amended bill would silence dissent and add to the federal government's assault on freedom of speech.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: That still stands.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Also important to note that I think Intro 553 duplicates an existing underutilized state program for cameras for small businesses.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And I do want to take the moment to thank all of the dozens and dozens of staffers that make a budget possible.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And thank you to my team as well.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Councilmember Morano, followed by Mealy.

chapters.summary: Council Member Morano honors Dorothy Day, whose image hangs in City Hall, and John Hudson Dilgen, a young man with epidermolysis bullosa who dedicated his life to giving hope. He reflects on their shared grace and compassion as Staten Islanders a century apart.
chapters.title: Morano on Dorothy Day and John Hudson Dilgen street co-namings

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Thank you, Majority Leader.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I know I don't often spend much time talking about street namings, especially on a day when we're considering so many important pieces of legislation, but I hope you'll indulge me for just a moment,

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: because today's package, Intro 859A, includes two street namings that mean an extraordinary amount to me and to the community that I represent.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: One honors Dorothy Day, a woman whose compassion, faith, and commitment to serving others changed countless lives.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: It's fitting that her image hangs here in City Hall, overlooking the staircase that so many of us walk each day.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: It's a quiet reminder that public service is ultimately about serving others.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And the other honors a young man gone far too soon, John Hudson Dilgen, a remarkable young man who lived with an unimaginable pain because of epidermolysis bullosa, yet never allowed his condition to define him.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Instead, he dedicated his life to giving hope to others, advancing research, and showing all of us what courage and resilience truly look like.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: These two Staten Islanders lived a century apart, and they led very different lives, but they shared something profound.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: They made our borough, our city, and our world better simply by the way they lived.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: If sainthood is ultimately for the church to decide, I can certainly say that in the eyes of so many Staten Islanders, these are two people whose lives reflected extraordinary grace, compassion, and selflessness.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I want to sincerely thank all of my colleagues for supporting these street namings and helping make sure their legacies will live on for generations to come.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Long after we're all gone, New Yorkers will pass these signs, ask who these people were, and learn their stories, and that's a powerful thing.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Today, we're forever memorializing not just names, but lives of service, compassion, courage, and hope.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: It's a privilege to have played a small part in that, and I'm deeply grateful to all of you for making that possible.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Councilor Mealy, followed by Aviles.

chapters.summary: Council Member Mealy thanks the Speaker and budget team, then highlights the co-naming of HL Apostolic Mecca Way for Greater Bible Way Temple, which has served Crown Heights for over 80 years, and Reverend Clinton M. Miller Way.
chapters.title: Mealy on street co-namings in Crown Heights

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: I want to thank the speaker, and then for her leadership with this budget.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: I want to thank all the budget team for doing such a great job.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: And I just want to thank all my community for our Old Street naming we just had last Saturday, the co-naming of the southeast corner of Rochester Avenue and Lincoln Place as HL

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Apostolic Mecca Way.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: I want to thank Apostle U.L.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Rogers, Reverend Herbert Daughtry, Teamster President, President Ford, District Leader Anthony T.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Jones for coming out that day.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: This co-naming is for more than 80 years.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: The Greater Bible Way Temple has been a cornerstone of Crown Heights, located on Rochester Avenue for over three decades.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: This church has been more than just a place of worship.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: It has been a place of refuge, service, and community care.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: The church has consistently met the real needs of the community.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Its Bible Way Learning Center also educated local children for decades, helping to shape professionals who continue to come back every year.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: This institution has uplifted generations through both faith and action, offering spiritual guidance alongside practical support.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: The co-naming of RL Apostolic Mecca Way reflects its legacy as a gathering place and a beacon of hope for the community it has served so faithfully.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: And I want to thank you for co-naming.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Reverend Clinton M.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Millerway, he has been a pillar of the community.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: I know it's not in my district.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: He has really did so much for us.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: And I want to thank Elam.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: They renamed his borough of Brooklyn Apostolic McKenzie Way.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: And he has been a...

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilman.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: A leader in our community.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Councilman Aviles, followed by Thomas Henry.

chapters.summary: Council Member Avilés honors principal Maria Interlandi Reno, Alzheimer's program leader Josephine Messicote Brown, Italian immigrant and market owner Paolo Pesce, and Fort Defiance from the Battle of Brooklyn's 250th anniversary.
chapters.title: Avilés on four street co-namings in District 3

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Thank you, colleagues.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Today I'm proud to bring four street co-namings to a vote that honor the leaders in history of District 3.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: First, we honor Maria Interlandi Reno, the beloved principal from PS 124, and her commitment to inclusive education.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: We also celebrate the life and work of Josephine Messicote Brown, the executive director of the New York Memory Center, who led the nation's first early intervention and support program for people diagnosed

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: with Alzheimer's and dementia.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: We also honor Paolo Pesce, an Italian immigrant and proud Bensonhurst resident who opened La Bella Marketplace, a family-owned market that is the hub for the community.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Finally, on the Battle of Brooklyn's 250th anniversary, we celebrate Red Hook's history by honoring Fort Defiance, a key site in the American

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Revolutionary War, and the terminus for the Lenape Trail later known as Red Heck Lane.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: I want to thank the family and friends and community members that led the charge to honor our district's history and community leaders.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Councilmember Thomas Henry, followed by Osei.

chapters.summary: Council Member Thomas-Henry honors Jimmy Smith, an Army veteran and community leader who served until age 95; Grace V. Lawrence, a founding member of numerous Queens institutions who died days before her 99th birthday; and Donica Cook, killed by a drunk driver 10 years ago today, whose mother started a foundation for reckless driving awareness.
chapters.title: Thomas-Henry on three street co-namings in District 21

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Today I am honored to recognize three individuals whose names will live on through District 21.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: The Honorable Jimmy Smith was an Army veteran, retired transit worker, former district leader, and community leader who served as a member of the East Elmhurst Corona Civic Association,

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: the chair of Elm Corps Youth and Adult Activities, and also served as chair of Community Board 3 and its budget committee where he served until his passing at the ripe age of 95.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Grace V.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Lawrence was a pillar throughout District 21, not only serving on numerous boards throughout the district, but rather as a founding member of so many institutions throughout my district, including the Langston Hughes Library Action Committee,

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Neighborhood Housing Services of Northern Queens, the Jerome Hardiman Senior Daycare Center, and the Louis Armstrong House Museum.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: She tirelessly worked throughout the community until passing just days before her 99th birthday.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: And last but certainly not least, I would like to honor the life of a young soul taken far too early by a drunk driver.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Donica Cook.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: was taken from us exactly 10 years ago today.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: She was a young woman whose life was just beginning, and she brought so much to so many.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: And since her passing, her mom, Julianne,

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: started a foundation to raise awareness regarding reckless driving.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: So she took that tragedy and turned it into a fight to fight for others so they would not have to suffer such a tragedy.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: So today, in honor of the 10-year anniversary of her passing, I want to say to her mom, the fight has been long.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: But it has come in time, and she will always be remembered throughout our community.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Councilmember Olse, followed by Felice.

chapters.summary: Council Member Ossé speaks about the street co-naming for Grammy Award-winning writer and producer Theodore 'Teddy' Vann, noting to his surprise that it is his grandfather. He thanks Council Member Joseph for sponsoring the co-naming.
chapters.title: Ossé on Teddy Vann street co-naming - his grandfather

utterances.speaker: Chi A. Ossé
utterances.text: Thank you, Majority Leader.

utterances.speaker: Chi A. Ossé
utterances.text: I want to speak about a street co-naming that's actually not in my district.

utterances.speaker: Chi A. Ossé
utterances.text: It's in Councilmember Joseph's district for Theodore Teddy Vann.

utterances.speaker: Chi A. Ossé
utterances.text: Teddy Vann was a Grammy Award-winning writer, producer, and humanitarian.

utterances.speaker: Chi A. Ossé
utterances.text: He wrote the 1960s hit Loop-de-Loop for Johnny Thunder and would win a Grammy for his song The Power of Love slash Love Power, a hit for Luther Vandross in 1991.

utterances.speaker: Chi A. Ossé
utterances.text: He had over 140 songs registered at Broadcast Music Incorporated, and he participated in a number of music projects, including Tales.

utterances.speaker: Chi A. Ossé
utterances.text: of the Dixie Drifter, which featured Paul McCartney, John Lennon, Sam Cooke, and Bob Dylan.

utterances.speaker: Chi A. Ossé
utterances.text: I really want to shout out Councilmember Joseph for this street co-naming.

utterances.speaker: Chi A. Ossé
utterances.text: I was surprised to see it on this agenda because this is my grandfather.

utterances.speaker: Chi A. Ossé
utterances.text: So thank you very much, Councilmember Joseph.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Anyone else signed up to speak for general orders?

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Did I miss anyone for general orders?

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: All right, I see Council Member Feliz may have stepped out, so he'll get to his remarks later.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Report of special committees.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: None.
